title_en: Commercial Transactions and the Role of Custom
content_en:
The Mudawwanah's chapters on commercial transactions reveal what may be the Maliki school's most juristically significant contribution to Islamic legal theory: the formal recognition of local custom ('urf and 'adah) as an independent source of legal rulings. While all schools acknowledge custom in practice, Malik elevated it to a recognized legal principle in ways that gave Maliki fiqh particular flexibility in commercial contexts.

The foundational Maliki position on bay' (sale) requires that both parties understand what they are exchanging and that the goods and price be specified. Malik's school inherited the Prophet's prohibition of gharar (excessive uncertainty) and applied it carefully to commercial transactions. The Mudawwanah records extensive discussion of which kinds of uncertainty invalidate a sale and which are acceptable as ordinary commercial ambiguity that custom tolerates.

Custom enters Maliki commercial law at multiple points. When a contract is silent on a matter that local practice has settled, Maliki fiqh fills the gap with what 'urf dictates. If merchants in a particular city consistently understand 'delivery' to mean something specific, Maliki judges will interpret ambiguous contracts according to that understanding. This principle made Maliki fiqh especially adaptable as it spread across diverse commercial cultures in Africa and Andalusia.

The Mudawwanah addresses the prohibition of riba with characteristic Maliki thoroughness. Malik accepted the well-known categories of riba al-fadl (surplus in exchange of the same commodity) and riba al-nasi'ah (riba through delay), but he also recognized a category of riba al-gharar in which uncertainty about delivery created an impermissible element of exchange. His treatment of ribawi goods — gold, silver, wheat, barley, dates, and salt — follows the six goods named in the relevant hadith.

On partnerships (sharikah and mudharabah), the Mudawwanah presents Malik's positions in considerable detail. Mudharabah — the sleeping partnership in which one party provides capital and another provides labor and management — was a common commercial arrangement in the early Islamic world, and Malik developed rules governing profit sharing, loss allocation, and the partner's liability. The Maliki school generally required that profit shares be specified as fractions and prohibited guaranteeing the capital partner against any loss.

Agricultural contracts receive special attention in the Mudawwanah given Madinah's date-palm economy. Musaqah — the contract by which an owner allows a worker to tend orchards in exchange for a share of the produce — was validated by Malik on the basis of the Prophet's own practice at Khaybar. This stands in contrast to the stricter Hanafi position that found musaqah problematic.

The Mudawwanah's commercial chapters also address the role of the market inspector (muhtasib) in enforcing fair trading. Malik believed that the state had both the right and duty to intervene in markets where traders were engaging in price manipulation, hoarding, or deceptive practices. This reflects the broader Maliki principle that public interest (maslahah) can inform legal rulings even without a specific textual basis — an idea Malik's student al-Shatibi would later develop into a systematic theory of the maqasid al-shari'ah.
